speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Chaya Rosen. Chaya is spelled C-H-A-Y-A. Last name is Rosen, R-O-S-E-N. And I am with Tama Fineberg. Today's date is July 28, 1996. And we are in Portland, Maine. Tama, can you please tell us the name you were given at birth and how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Tama, T-A-M-A. And Hachman, H-A-C-H-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Mlynow. And it was Poland, I believe, at the time, May 1, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rochel, and my father's name was Leib.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came from the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother was born in Riga, Latvia. And as a very young bride of 16, my father brought her to Mlynow, where he was a little bit older and an officer in the Russian Army. Madly in love, they sorry each other and that was, that was it. And they got married, and she lived in Mlynow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very skilled carpenter. He had, my mother always said, hands of gold. He could make very intricate wonderful things. And he used to do apprentice work, have children come to him, and they would learn from him. And this is how he made his living. And he also built the house that we lived in and he made all the furniture that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the youngest of six. And do you want me to give you the name of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers. I'll put on my glasses, I've reached that age.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother's name was Shaya, then I had a sister. You'll forgive me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was Shaya, [SOBBING] and Malla, Fega, and Efraim, Mayer, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately how much older they were than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe my mother said she had every two years. She had a child every two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were born in 1937, you all lived in the same house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all lived in the same house. And we didn't have all these bedrooms that we have now. And for some reason, the house was happy and warm and Shabbat came along and everybody knew what they had to do. And being the baby, I was being taken care of by my oldest sister Malla. And set the Shabbat table and she'd make-- my mother was a wonderful cook, a wonderful cook. And then Daddy would come home and short time, short time it lasted for me because then everything was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, was your family orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very orthodox. They'd go to Shabbat. Everybody went to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your house looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a large dining room table. I don't know if was a dining room or a kitchen, but I know it was a large table because all the kids and my parents would sit around the table and we would have our dinner. And mother was in one end, and my father was on the other end, and the children on each side. That's the best that I can recollect at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you were young, but do you remember anything else about your life before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a poor life. But I didn't know any different. And it was a happy life because there was a lot of love in the house. The children all seemed to do their work and do what had to be done. And I was probably a little bit spoiled because I was the baby and everybody catered to me. And my brothers used to make little things for me to play with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to make like a little doll that one of my brothers carved out a wood. And my mother sewed a little dress. And that was my doll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your brothers and sisters going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember them going to [YIDDISH]. The boys went to [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, just this-- whether it was the afternoon or the morning, whenever the sessions were at that time. But I remember them. If I would ask my mother, where is my brother Mayer or Efraim-- he hasn't come home from [YIDDISH] yet, she'd always say to me. Just be patient, they'll be here soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were home with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any ways that you spent the day with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go to the market. And she used to for Shabbat. That's the thing that stands out the most. And she'd buy the chicken. And at that time, you buy a live chicken. And then she had to go to a place where they would kill the chicken. And she used to have to make me stay outside because she didn't want me to see what they did. And then she'd come home and she'd flick the chicken and get it ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any sense of a community or a neighborhood where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close, very close community. I-- my father's sister lived directly across the street from us. And they too had six children. And I remember being over there a lot. We played with one another. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very warm, warm feeling with everybody. The love was always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that the families around you were, were Jewish, or were they mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. Most of them around me were all Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any-- did your family have any interactions with the non-Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father because he used to take on apprentice. And a lot of the non-Jewish people who had little farms outside our little shtetl, a lot of their kids used to come and work and learn from my father. In return, they would bring things that their mothers would make, like jams and butter and, you know, things like that, instead of pay, because they too didn't have it. But it was an exchange of things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a friendly exchange?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very friendly and very warm. And they just loved him because he was so good at what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before the war, your remembrances of your childhood were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, it didn't last long.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was some of the early indications to you as a young child that things were changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, it was like a rumble going on. Everybody was whispering something is going to come about. And like I told you, my father was in the military, so he felt we have to take hold and get a group together and stand strong. And not just stand around and talk about it, let's do something. If something does come, let's try and fight them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your father was an officer in the Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was an officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he, when he would say that, would he say it to other Jews or mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Just the Jewish people, a lot of the neighbors. They would get together and they would like talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would hear this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I Would hear this because they would be in the house and they would discuss it that something is going on. But I didn't know, I was too young to really understand. It was just things that I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did those discussions last long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the night, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, how often did they occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, a few times, I guess, that I can recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what, what happened next, as far as you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, it came through what they suspected, that the Germans were going to get after the Jewish people, annihilate us, I guess, was their goal. And next thing you knew, they said that they were putting barbed wires around our little shtetl in Mlynow. And I remember them rolling these huge, huge-- it looked like a barrel but, probably like something you see cable wire go on. These huge things, I remember them pushing along the streets. And it was barbed wire and they surrounded the whole little shtetl with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who was doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there were notices sent out that Juden, meaning, in German it means Jew, are to be-- all of us had to wear a yellow star sewed on to our outer garments. And at no time are we to go outside without that yellow star. And we had to stay within the ghetto that they surrounded. And nobody was allowed to go outside anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen if somebody did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would get shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard them say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard them say it. I saw them do it, because somebody tried to go and they killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the neighbors that lived not far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: A young woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any reaction to this episode from the other people who lived nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Panic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Panic by the Jewish families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the non-Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know about them, I have no idea about them. They-- I should preface this by saying to you that just before they surrounded, my mother took two of my brothers, Mayer and Efraim, and she was able to get them out and had them go to the so-called good, non-Jewish people that we knew, thinking that if anything was to happen to us they would have a better chance because they would be outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how old they were, your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe eight and 10, or eight and six. I'm not certain of their age. You know, I'm trying to talk, it's over 50 years of trying to suppress everything into my head so that I don't remember it because it was so hard to live with. And since you people contacted me, I've been trying to go back and I'm just, you know, trying to bring, so please bear with me. I-- I'm do the best I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who would you say organized the fencing around the ghettos? Would you say they're mostly German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they would get some of the people in the shtetl to help them do the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were those people? Were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of our Jewish neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they place any of those people in charge of, of this operation? Any of the Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones in charge were the ones with the rifles on their shoulders. And they said, you will do such and such. And they did it. I mean, it was-- they did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued being able to stay in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes, we were in the house. And my father knew that no good was going to come out of this. So as I told you, he was a very skilled carpenter. He had this beautiful hutch that he had in our house. And he took that hutch and opened up the bottom part of the floor and he made it look so that when you close it, you'd never know it was able to open. He took that out, dug a hole underneath a small area. And he always said to my mother, something is going to happen. And when it does, I don't know if I will be here. I want you to take the children and go down. And it's like he knew something was going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came in the middle of the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, because they knew that he wanted to get some people together to fight against this. And they considered him an instigator. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they took-- they came in the middle of the night. They broke the door open. They came in and they grabbed him. And I believe that's when he was shot that time, because they, they weren't about to tolerate anybody that was going to try and do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that night because I remember everybody screaming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother was crying. [SOB] And my father said, [SOBBING] remember what I told you, in Yiddish. She said, I will. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was it. I never saw him again. [CRYING] By that time, two of my brothers out of the city. So and my sister, one of my sisters, had died. So it was just my older sister, Malla, my mother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as a child, you know, you forget quickly. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran across the street to my cousin's house, which I frequent constantly because I loved being there. And my mother said to my sister Malla, go get Tama, bring her to me. And she did, she went over and took me. And like 10 minutes later, a big truck drove up, Germans, and they piled everybody out from that house onto the truck. If she hadn't taken me out, I would have been one of them on that truck. And of course, they took them away. That's when they started gathering them in trucks and driving away from the town into the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I think '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember possibly what month or time of the year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the Fall, I'm not certain. I just remember a lot of screaming, a lot of crying. And my mother, when she heard that, she took my sister and I and we went down where my father told us to go. And we sat there and closed the lid down. He made it so that you'd never know if you opened the cabinet that that lid opened up. And we sat there, and I guess time went on. It must have been night time or date, whatever. Because we heard Germans coming into our house and keine Juden, nicht keine Juden, Du. And they kept saying that over and over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that there doesn't seem to be any Jews here. Where are they, where are the Jews? And they kept looking and searching. And then a lot of them started taking things because we heard them taking our belongings. And they would take whatever they wanted at that time. And they just kept-- I heard the footsteps, footsteps. And then after a while, it got very quiet. And we kept sitting there. And my mother kept pleading with me not to say anything because I was so young. And then she thought maybe she'll take a chance and take a look and see what's happening. And she walked up and opened the lid from the cabinet. And she peeked through and she didn't see anybody in the house, just rubble, you know, things that were thrown down. And the three of us came up. And I remember going through and she kept saying to me, please, don't, don't make a noise, don't make a noise. Because in the windows we could see those terrible helmets that the Germans were wearing and the tips of their rifles that I remembered, walking back and forth, back and forth. And she says, now, please, be very quiet. And she took my hand very tightly and my sister's, and we went through like the back doors. And through alleyways. And we were running and running until we get to the barbed wire. And she says, please. My sister started tried bending it apart so that we could crawl through. And as we did, my hat got caught on the barbed wire. She says, don't go back for it, we have to leave it. And that's when our running started. We just ran and ran and ran until we got away from that barbed wire. And we ran into the woods. And then I don't know what happened. My sister got separated from us. I don't know how it happened. But my mother kept looking for my sister. And my sister kept looking for us. And we went to all the different little farms that my mother knew these people. They used to come, you know, to us. And she'd say, did you see my daughter. And they'd said, she was just here, she was looking for you. And it was just like we kept missing each other, one after another. And then the last one that we finally went to said to us that yes, she had seen her. And by this time, she had given up all hope of us being alive and she said, if they killed them, [SOBBING] I don't want to live. She gave herself up and they killed her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was the end of my sister. [SOBBING] So it just left my mother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, do you recall if there was any question in anybody's mind if one of those people who opened the door to answer your-- your mother's question would have taken her in and hid her?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That's what we were hoping for. But she wouldn't stay because she kept searching for us. She would go from one farm to another farm, the ones, the people that we knew that she thought for sure we would be there. And she just kept going and going and going. And we kept following her, but could never catch up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know if they offered to help, the families?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said that they said they would keep her for a little bit, but she says, no, I have to look for my mother and my sister. And then like I said, she gave up all hope of finding us. She thought they got us. She figured, my mother running with a small child, they must have gotten us. So she just said, I can't live like this, I cannot be the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know possibly how old she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was my oldest sister. So she must have been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 15. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She must have been about that. I'm just guessing, I was really too young to remember all the ages and dates or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you did after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, constant running-- running and hiding and hiding and running. And we came to a cemetery place. And we used to hide there. And my mother prayed for rain so we could have something to drink. And at night, she would go searching to the farm places for something to eat. And I remember because they used to feed the pigs and they used to dump a bunch of slop out there. And she would hide around the corner. She knew when this would happen. And she would grab that slop, whatever she could, and tried to feed me and herself.
